content.assessment: This proposal has the strongest and most direct comparative basis of the six. Cross-national econometric evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann) explicitly names Hungary as an early-tracking country and finds early tracking increases inequality with a tendency to reduce mean performance (F-live-international_comparison-04, moderate), directly supporting the proposal's rationale. Finland's 1972-77 delay from age 11 to 16 is a genuine comparable mechanism (statutory delay of first tracking), but the evidence is explicit that the reform worked only because it was bundled with ability-grouping abolition and systemic curriculum/teacher-training change (F-live-international_comparison-08), and even then produced only modest long-run gains. Poland offers a comparable case of tracking-age change, but its 2017 reversal (moving tracking earlier, without phasing) caused severe capacity shortfalls (F-live-international_comparison-07), which is directly relevant as a transition-design warning for any Hungarian delay, particularly the reverse-direction risk of insufficient phasing. Crucially, Hungary's context differs from these comparators in two consequential ways not present in Finland or Germany: entangled ethnic/Roma segregation (F-live-international_comparison-06) and the tracking-choice interaction (F-live-international_comparison-09), meaning delay alone, unbundled from choice regulation, has uncertain transferability even if the Finnish bundling lesson is heeded.
opportunities: The Polish reversal case offers actionable transition-design lessons (explicit phasing, capacity planning) that could be built directly into implementation sequencing
strengths: Direct, named cross-national econometric evidence supports the core causal claim that early tracking (as practiced in Hungary) increases inequality and can reduce mean performance (F-live-international_comparison-04)

Finland provides an actual comparable mechanism—statutory delay of first tracking from age-equivalent 11 to 16—demonstrating this exact policy lever has been implemented and evaluated (F-live-international_comparison-08)

Poland provides a directly relevant transition-design case study, showing what happens when tracking-age changes are not carefully phased (F-live-international_comparison-07), which the proposal explicitly attempts to design around
threats: Hungary's ethnic/Roma segregation dimension is structurally distinct from all cited comparator countries (F-live-international_comparison-06), meaning even a well-bundled and well-phased delay modeled on Finland/Poland could fail to address the specific segregation channel most salient in the Hungarian case

Hungary's between-school variance is already the third-highest in the OECD (F-live-international_comparison-02) and its socioeconomic gap the largest (F-live-international_comparison-03), suggesting the starting-point severity may exceed anything in the Finnish or Polish reform contexts, complicating outcome-relevance extrapolation from those cases
weaknesses: Finland's positive effects were 'modest' and contingent on bundling with ability-grouping abolition and teacher-training reform; delay alone (unbundled) has no clean comparative precedent of success (F-live-international_comparison-08)

Hungary's tracking-choice interaction is a bundled institutional feature 'not present in the same form' in Germany or Nordic comprehensive systems, meaning even a successfully bundled Finnish-style delay may not transfer if free choice remains unregulated (F-live-international_comparison-09)
